comments.text: GPT-5.5 Pro says the statement of Problem #708 appears to have a wording issue: the condition\[ |B|=g(n) \]should likely be\[ |B|\le g(n), \]or equivalently \(g(n)\) should be defined as the worst-case minimum size of such a set \(B\). Evidence: take\[ A=\{2,3,\ldots,n+1\},\qquad M=n+1,\qquad I=\{1,2,\ldots,n+1\}. \]Then\[ \prod_{a\in A}a=(n+1)! = \prod_{b\in I}b, \]so \(B=I\) works. But every \(B\subseteq I\) has\[ |B|\le |I|=n+1. \]Thus, if the statement literally requires \(|B|=g(n)\) for every legal \(A,I\), then this example forces \(g(n)\le n+1\). That conflicts with the statement that Erdős and Surányi proved\[ g(n)\ge (2-o(1))n. \]So the intended formulation is presumably:\[ g(n)=\sup_{A,I}\min\left\{|B|:B\subseteq I,\ \prod_{a\in A}a\mid\prod_{b\in B}b\right\}, \]or: there exists \(B\subseteq I\) with \(|B|\le g(n)\).
